industry_reference.source_name: American Roller Bearing — Common Causes of Bearing Failure
industry_reference.summary_text: General mechanical-engineering knowledge: overload / overstrain failures occur when loads exceed design limits, over-stressing rolling elements, raceways and structural members. Excess contact pressure and uneven stress distribution deform surfaces, initiate micro-cracks and drive subsurface fatigue that shows up as flaking/spalling, shortening life. Stalls, jams, misalignment and inadequate lubrication are common overload triggers.

root_cause_text: Roughing Mill Stand R2's work rolls lost profile through the campaign: abrasive contact with hot, scaled stock caused roll-surface wear and localized spalling, degrading the bar and its surface before the scheduled change. Extended run time between grinds was the main driver.
corrective_action: Replaced/reground the affected rolls and moved to wear- and tonnage-based roll-change scheduling with in-mill profile checks, rather than purely time-based changes.
violated_step: Step 2 of PROC-024 ("Allow residual stock to clear before de-energising the main drive.") — the wear-inspection interval was exceeded before the event.

industry_reference.summary_text: General tribology knowledge: working surfaces lose material progressively through abrasive wear (hard particles ploughing/grooving the surface), adhesive wear (asperities bonding then shearing as surfaces slide) and surface fatigue. Wear rate rises with contact load, sliding speed, temperature and abrasive contamination and falls with surface hardness — so rolls, mandrels, tools and belts in contact with hard or abrasive stock wear predictably and must be reground/replaced on condition.

root_cause_text: On the Runout Table Cooling System, strip coiling temperature crept above target because a bank of laminar headers delivered reduced flow — nozzle scaling and a sticking flow-control valve cut water to the top headers, so the strip was under-cooled across part of the table.
corrective_action: Cleared the scaled header nozzles, overhauled the flow-control valve, and added header-bank flow monitoring tied to the coiling-temperature model so an under-delivering bank is detected immediately.
violated_step: Step 1 of PROC-002 ("Allow the cooling bed to cool to a safe inspection temperature and confirm atmosphere is safe to enter or approach.") — cooling/lubrication verification was not completed at the required interval before the event.

industry_reference.source_name: Dacpol — Industrial Equipment Overheating: Common Causes and Solutions
industry_reference.summary_text: General engineering knowledge: heat-dissipation / overheating failures arise when a cooling system can no longer remove heat as fast as the process generates it — commonly from fouled or scaled heat-exchange surfaces, restricted airflow, clogged or leaking coolant passages, degraded coolant, or worn pumps reducing circulation. As heat extraction falls below the generated load, component temperatures climb past their rated band and thermal damage follows.

industry_reference.summary_text: General tribology knowledge: working surfaces lose material progressively through abrasive wear (hard particles ploughing/grooving the surface), adhesive wear (asperities bonding then shearing as surfaces slide) and surface fatigue. Wear rate rises with contact load, sliding speed, temperature and abrasive contamination and falls with surface hardness — so rolls, mandrels, tools and belts in contact with hard or abrasive stock wear predictably and must be reground/replaced on condition.

root_cause_text: Electric Arc Furnace #1 tripped on a power-delivery fault: the electrode-regulation drive drew overcurrent as arc impedance swung, pointing to an unstable supply interacting with the regulation loop rather than a mechanical cause. A marginal electrode contact and supply dips triggered the trip.

violated_step: Step 3 of PROC-005 ("Check sensor and instrumentation calibration against reference values.") — the electrical isolation/calibration check was missed in the prior PM cycle.

industry_reference.source_name: Precision Electric — VFD Overcurrent Fault & Motor Overload: Causes & Fixes
industry_reference.summary_text: General electrical-engineering knowledge: drive/motor power failures commonly stem from overcurrent conditions — ground faults, winding-to-winding or phase-to-ground shorts from insulation breakdown, or mechanical overload reflected back into the motor. Voltage spikes and PWM harmonics from variable-frequency drives accelerate insulation and bearing wear, and an unstable supply or too-short acceleration ramp can trip or damage the drive.

root_cause_text: Continuous Casting Machine #1 tripped on segment overload: a withdrawal/strand-guide roll bound under load, driving segment forces above their limit during casting. Bearing wear and a misaligned roll concentrated stress on the segment, and the drive saw the reflected overload.
corrective_action: Replaced the seized roll and its bearings, re-aligned the affected segment, and added segment load-cell trending to catch a binding roll before it overloads the strand.
violated_step: Step 3 of PROC-009 ("Isolate power and apply lockout-tagout, then bleed hydraulic and pneumatic pressure to zero.") — operating-limit verification was not performed prior to the event.

industry_reference.source_name: American Roller Bearing — Common Causes of Bearing Failure
industry_reference.summary_text: General mechanical-engineering knowledge: overload / overstrain failures occur when loads exceed design limits, over-stressing rolling elements, raceways and structural members. Excess contact pressure and uneven stress distribution deform surfaces, initiate micro-cracks and drive subsurface fatigue that shows up as flaking/spalling, shortening life. Stalls, jams, misalignment and inadequate lubrication are common overload triggers.

root_cause_text: Hot Blast Stove B tripped in a way that did not resolve to a single deterministic cause: sensor traces were within normal bands before the event and no refractory, valve or burner defect was found. It is classified as a random failure, most consistent with a transient external disturbance during otherwise-normal useful-life operation.
corrective_action: Reset and returned the stove to service under enhanced monitoring and scheduled a confirmatory inspection; no design change is indicated for a one-off random event.
violated_step: Step 4 of PROC-008 ("Record final temperatures and any anomalies in the shift log.") — the routine monitoring entry was missing from the shift log prior to the event.

industry_reference.summary_text: General reliability-engineering knowledge: during the 'useful life' (flat) region of the bathtub curve, equipment shows a low, roughly constant failure rate driven by random external stressors — power surges, physical impacts, environmental fluctuations, operator error or occasional undetected defects — rather than by age or wear-out. Such failures are stochastic and not attributable to a single deterministic root cause.

violated_step: Step 3 of PROC-020 ("Check sensor and instrumentation calibration against reference values.") — the electrical isolation/calibration check was missed in the prior PM cycle.

root_cause_text: Root cause was excessive tool wear: accumulated tool-wear minutes exceeded the qualified service life, degrading process capability and surface quality before scheduled replacement. Extended run time between changes was the main driver.
corrective_action: Replaced the worn tool and implemented tool-wear-based (rather than purely time-based) replacement scheduling.
violated_step: Step 2 of PROC-026 ("Inspect bearings, seals, couplings and drive components for wear, and top up or replace lubricants as specified.") — the wear-inspection interval was exceeded before the event.

root_cause_text: Root cause identified as mechanical overstrain: the product of tool wear and applied torque exceeded the overstrain threshold for the material variant being processed. Worn tooling combined with high torque demand drove the strain beyond safe limits.
corrective_action: Replaced worn tooling, re-balanced feed and speed set-points, and tightened the tool-wear replacement interval.
violated_step: Step 3 of PROC-024 ("Isolate power and apply lockout-tagout, then bleed hydraulic and pneumatic pressure to zero.") — operating-limit verification was not performed prior to the event.
